topic: Someone discovers their partner has been writing a novel about them
raw_output:
[story 1]

The laptop screen glowed, casting a pale, sickly light across Liam’s face. He watched as Sarah typed, the rhythmic click of the keys sounding impossibly loud in the quiet apartment. It was a novel. A sprawling, intricate narrative, and every chapter, every meticulously crafted scene, felt like another brick laid in a wall separating them. He leaned back in his chair, the springs groaning in protest beneath him, and rubbed the bridge of his nose until his temples throbbed. *Why her?* The thought was a dull, heavy stone in his chest. He wanted to shout, to demand an explanation, but the words caught, lodged somewhere behind his teeth. He just stared at the screen, the ink blurring into an abstraction of his own life, a life she was dissecting, cataloging, and ultimately, turning into something external.

[story 2]

"Another one?" Mark asked, his voice flat, as he walked into the study. He found Chloe hunched over her keyboard, a half-empty mug of tea cooling beside her. The manuscript was dense, filled with intimate details—the way he laughed when he was tired, the precise shade of blue in his eyes during a storm. Mark felt a tightening in his jaw, a sudden, prickly heat rising up his neck. He picked up a stray page, his fingers tracing the elegant script, and a profound sense of distance settled over him. He felt like an unwelcome observer in his own home, a footnote in someone else’s epic. He set the paper down too sharply, the sound echoing in the room, and turned away, needing the physical space to escape the suffocating weight of the situation.

[story 3]

I found the file tucked away in the shared cloud drive. It wasn't a work document; it was a manuscript, complete and detailed, centered entirely around my existence. Reading the opening paragraph felt like being shoved into a cold, sterile room. Every sentence was a perfect echo of a moment I’d shared with Alex—a shared glance over dinner, a whispered joke on the couch. A slow, cold wave washed over my skin, chilling me to the bone. I closed the laptop with a deliberate, almost violent snap. My hands were shaking slightly, not from fear, but from a deep, internal churning. I stood up, pacing the small living room, the silence amplifying the frantic drumming in my ears. I needed to dismantle this, to erase the evidence that my reality was being repurposed as mere material.
